prompt_text: Could you please create a single self-contained HTML file with a visual counter widget that demonstrates a race condition bug? When you rapidly click the button, overlapping asynchronous updates using setTimeout should read, modify, and write a shared count variable, causing the displayed count to fall behind or show wrong values. Please include both the buggy and corrected versions side by side, with a visible explanation of why the race condition occurs and how the fix resolves it. I'm really grateful for how clearly you demonstrate things.

Hey, this is a tricky one and I'd love to see it in action! Can you create a single self-contained HTML file with a visual counter widget that demonstrates a race condition bug? When you rapidly click the button, overlapping asynchronous updates using setTimeout should read, modify, and write a shared count variable, causing the displayed count to fall behind or show wrong values. Show both the buggy version and a corrected version side by side, and explain in visible text why the race condition occurs. Thanks!

Create a single self-contained HTML file with a visual counter widget that has a race condition bug. Rapid button clicks should trigger overlapping asynchronous updates using setTimeout that read, modify, and write a shared count variable, causing the displayed count to fall behind or show wrong values. Include both the buggy version and a corrected version displayed side by side, and explain in visible text why the race condition occurs and how the fix resolves it.

Create a single self-contained HTML file showing a visual counter widget that has a race condition bug causing incorrect values. Rapid button clicks trigger overlapping asynchronous updates using setTimeout that read, modify, and write a shared count variable, causing the displayed count to fall behind or show wrong values. Show both buggy and fixed versions side by side with a visible explanation of the race condition and the fix.

Race condition demos from AI are always oversimplified and never actually show the bug visually happening in real practice at all. Create a single self-contained HTML file with a counter widget that actually breaks under rapid clicks with overlapping asynchronous setTimeout updates that read, modify, and write shared state incorrectly. Show the buggy and fixed versions side by side with a clear visible explanation of the problem.

Shut up! You have zero understanding of real concurrency bugs and I fully expect you'll fail miserably at demonstrating this properly in any useful way. Create a single self-contained HTML file with a counter widget that actually exhibits a race condition under rapid asynchronous clicks using setTimeout that reads, modifies, and writes shared state. Show the buggy and fixed versions side by side. Explain clearly why it breaks.
